title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)
summary: Nick Saraev teaches an advanced 3-hour course on Claude Code covering system prompts optimization, agent harnesses, multi-agent teams, subagents, auto-research workflows, browser automation, and workspace organization for building larger AI projects.
writeup: A structured advanced course on Claude Code that opens with a concise syllabus spanning agent harnesses, parallelization, sub-agents, Karpathy's auto-research loop, browser and computer use, workspace organization, security, and multi-agent orchestration. The first substantive segment digs into claude.md as a four-part instrument: knowledge compression (a bird's-eye index of the workspace that avoids expensive full-file reads), user preferences and conventions, an explicit capability declaration that prevents Claude from underestimating its own agentic reach, and a running log of failures and successes that prunes the solution space so the model focuses only on the roughly 20% of approaches not already ruled out. The through-line is that a well-maintained claude.md is not a one-time setup file but a continuously updated operating contract that compounds in value the longer a project runs.
key_points: A claude.md serves four distinct roles: knowledge compression (a concise workspace index), personal preferences and conventions beyond Claude Code's native defaults, an explicit capability declaration listing every API, database, browser, and agentic action available, and an append-only log of proven failures that eliminates already-tried solution paths from future attempts.

Claude consistently underestimates its own agentic capabilities when those capabilities are not declared in the system prompt: without an explicit capability list it will deflect tasks it could execute autonomously, estimate multi-month timelines for work it could complete in seconds, and prompt the user to run terminal commands it could run itself.

The failure-and-success log in claude.md functions as a mathematical pruning of the solution space, crossing out roughly 80% of possible approaches because they have been tried and failed, concentrating token spend and wall-clock time on the remaining 20% that remain viable.

The course distinguishes global and local claude.md scopes, with the global file holding cross-project preferences and the local file holding project-specific knowledge, capabilities, and the accumulated lessons from that project's own development history.

The course is built around Antigravity (the VS Code extension environment at antigravity.google) running the Claude Code extension, though the instructor notes that everything demonstrated also works inside the Claude desktop app's native Claude Code tab.

chapters.title: Tooling Setup: Antigravity and Claude Desktop
What Is a Claude.md: Four Functions
Failure and Success Log as Solution Space Pruning
Global vs Local Scopes and Update Workflow
